section.title: The Waters of Marah
section.sectionRationale: Only three days after singing at the sea, Israel grumbles over bitter water. This short scene introduces the wilderness pattern that will repeat again and again: need, complaint, God's provision, and a test of whether Israel will trust and obey.
section.thesis: Three days into the wilderness with no water, then bitter water they cannot drink, Israel grumbles against Moses; the LORD sweetens the water through a log He shows Moses, tests His people there, and reveals Himself as the LORD who heals, before leading them to abundant rest at Elim.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the bitter water at Marah, God's provision, and His covenant test and promise; and the rest and abundance found afterward at Elim.

verses.text: Then Moses led Israel from the Red Sea, and they went out into the Desert of Shur. For three days they walked in the desert without finding water.
And when they came to {{marah|Marah}}, they could not drink the water there because it was bitter. (That is why it was named Marah.)
So the people grumbled against Moses, saying, “What are we to drink?”

And Moses cried out to the LORD, and the LORD showed him a log. And when he cast it into the waters, they were sweetened. 
There the LORD made for them a statute and an ordinance, and there He tested them,

saying, “If you will listen carefully to the voice of the LORD your God, and do what is right in His eyes, and pay attention to His commands, and keep all His statutes, then I will not bring on you any of the diseases I inflicted on the Egyptians. For I am the LORD who {{heals|heals}} you.”
Then they came to Elim, where there were twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees, and they camped there by the waters.

marah.gloss: Bitter. The place is named for the taste of its water, but the word also echoes Israel's bitterness of spirit in grumbling so soon after singing at the sea, only three days before.

heals.gloss: To heal, to make whole. The LORD names Himself here Yahweh Rapha, the LORD who heals, tying His covenant faithfulness directly to bodily wholeness and, by implication, to every kind of restoration His people need.

basic.context: Just three days after singing God's praises at the sea, Israel hits its first real test: no water, then bitter water they cannot drink. They complain to Moses, and God sweetens the water through a simple log, then uses the moment to test them and reveal Himself as their healer.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real event in Israel's early wilderness wandering. It is not a magical formula about logs healing water, but a lesson about how quickly people who have seen great grace can grumble under small pressure, and about God's pattern of providing exactly what His people need while testing whether they will trust Him.
basic.originalLanguages: The place is named marah, bitter, for its undrinkable water, a fitting picture of Israel's own bitter complaint so soon after their song of praise. God then reveals Himself as the One who rapha, heals, promising that obedience keeps His people from the diseases inflicted on Egypt.
basic.typology: The bitter water made sweet by something cast into it echoes throughout Scripture's pattern of God transforming what is unusable into a source of life, later fulfilled when Christ turns bitterness, sin, and death itself into life for those united to Him.
basic.christ: God names Himself here the LORD who heals, a title that anticipates Christ's ministry of healing bodies as a sign of the greater healing He brings to sin-sick souls, and ultimately the full and final healing of all things in the new creation, Revelation 22:2.
basic.god: God is patient with a people who complain almost immediately after praising Him, meeting their grumbling not with abandonment but with provision. Yet He also uses ordinary hardship as a genuine test, wanting obedience that flows from trust, not mere memory of past deliverance.
basic.crossReferences: James 1:2-4 speaks of trials testing faith to produce endurance, echoing the testing at Marah. Revelation 22:1-2 pictures water and healing finally perfected in the new creation. Deuteronomy 8:2-3 recalls this wilderness testing as designed to reveal what was in Israel's heart.
basic.oneStory: Even freshly redeemed people grumble quickly when the next hardship comes, and God meets that grumbling not with rejection but with provision and a test of trust. This pattern, God's care meeting human weakness in the wilderness, points toward Christ, who leads His people through every wilderness to a rest and healing this world cannot give.

context.literary: The scene moves quickly from crisis to provision to covenant statement, a pattern that becomes the template for the wilderness narratives to follow, before ending on a note of abundance at Elim that briefly resolves the tension.
context.historical: This is Israel's first recorded trial after the exodus, establishing early the rhythm of complaint and provision that will recur throughout the wilderness wanderings in Exodus and Numbers.
context.canonical: The title the LORD who heals introduced here becomes part of Israel's ongoing confession about God's character, and the pattern of testing in the wilderness is picked up explicitly in Deuteronomy 8 and applied to spiritual formation throughout Scripture.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show how quickly Israel's trust could falter under hardship, and to establish early that the LORD both provides for His people's genuine needs and tests them to reveal and deepen their trust in Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage narrates a specific historical provision, sweetening bitter water through a log, and a specific covenant statement to Israel; it does not prescribe a general formula for healing today. Its enduring principle is the character of God as provider and healer and His pattern of testing faith through hardship, not the specific means used at Marah.

debates.question: How should the promise in verse 26, that obedience prevents the diseases of Egypt, be applied today?

views.label: A covenant promise specific to Israel under the old covenant
views.case: This is part of the conditional blessings and curses structure of the Mosaic covenant, later expanded fully in Deuteronomy 28, tied to Israel's national obedience, not a universal guarantee of physical health for individual believers.

views.label: A general principle of wisdom
views.case: Some see in it a broader wisdom pattern, that a life ordered by God's commands tends toward wellbeing, while cautioning against reading it as a promise that obedient people never suffer illness, since Scripture elsewhere shows righteous people suffering, as in Job.

debates.bearing: Both views agree this is not a formula guaranteeing health in exchange for obedience; it is a covenant statement about God's character and Israel's calling, read carefully alongside the rest of Scripture's more complex witness on suffering.

study.originalLanguages: The bitter water at marah gives the place its name, and the word's echo of Israel's own bitterness of complaint is likely deliberate. God's self-revelation as the LORD who rapha, heals, ties His covenant faithfulness to wholeness and restoration, a theme picked up throughout the rest of the Bible's story of redemption.

typology.fulfillment: A picture of God transforming what brings death into a source of life, fulfilled fully in Christ, who brings life out of death itself, John 11:25-26.

typology.type: The LORD who heals, Yahweh Rapha
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ's healing ministry and points toward the final healing of all things, when there is no more mourning or pain, Revelation 21:4, 22:2.

study.christ: The title revealed here, the LORD who heals, finds its clearest expression in Jesus, who heals the sick as a visible sign that God's kingdom has come to deal with sin's full effects on both body and soul. The pattern of bitter water made sweet anticipates the gospel's larger pattern: what was under a curse becomes, through Christ, a source of life.
study.god: God is patient with a people who grumble almost as soon as they have finished singing His praise, providing exactly what they need. Yet He is also a God who tests His people deliberately, not to trip them up but to reveal and strengthen genuine trust, and who reveals His own character progressively, here as healer, to a people just beginning to know Him.

crossReferences.note: Moses later explains that the LORD led Israel through the wilderness specifically to test and know what was in their heart.
Trials test faith to produce endurance, the same dynamic at work in Israel's testing at Marah.
The new creation pictures perfected water and healing, the ultimate fulfillment of the LORD who heals.
Praises the LORD who forgives all your iniquity and heals all your diseases, echoing this title's fuller meaning.

study.oneStory: Barely three days from their greatest deliverance, Israel grumbles at the first hardship, and God meets them not with rejection but with sweetened water, a test, and a promise. This is the pattern of the whole Bible's story: a God who patiently provides for and tests His often faithless people, until Christ leads His people through every wilderness to the final healing of all things.

quiz.q: How long had Israel gone without finding water before reaching Marah?

options.text: Three days.
options.note: Verse 22 states this precisely, only three days after the Red Sea crossing.

options.text: Forty days.
options.note: Forty is a significant later wilderness number, but not the figure given here; it is three days.

options.text: A full year.
options.note: The text specifies three days, a very short span after their great deliverance.

options.text: The text gives no length of time.
options.note: Verse 22 explicitly states three days.

quiz.q: How did the LORD make the bitter water drinkable?

options.text: He showed Moses a log, which Moses cast into the water, and it was sweetened.
options.note: Verse 25 describes this exact means of provision.

options.text: He commanded the people to boil the water first.
options.note: The text specifies the log cast into the water, not a boiling process.

options.text: He led them to a different water source entirely, bypassing Marah.
options.note: The text says the water at Marah itself was made sweet, not that they avoided it.

options.text: The water became drinkable on its own without any divine action.
options.note: The text credits the LORD directly with showing Moses the means of provision.

quiz.q: What did the people do when they first found the water at Marah bitter?

options.text: They grumbled against Moses, asking what they were to drink.
options.note: Verse 24 records their complaint directly.

options.text: They immediately trusted God to provide without complaint.
options.note: The text explicitly records grumbling, not calm trust, as their first response.

options.text: They returned to Egypt on their own.
options.note: There is no return to Egypt in this passage; they grumble, but stay and see God's provision.

options.text: They attacked Moses physically.
options.note: The text describes verbal grumbling, not a physical attack.

quiz.q: What title does the LORD give Himself in verse 26?

options.text: The LORD who heals you.
options.note: This is the exact title given, tied to Israel's obedience and God's covenant care.

options.text: The LORD who provides.
options.note: That title, Yahweh Yireh, comes from Genesis 22:14, a different passage, not this one.

options.text: The LORD our righteousness.
options.note: That title appears in Jeremiah 23:6, not in this passage.

options.text: No title is given in this passage.
options.note: Verse 26 explicitly gives the title, the LORD who heals you.

quiz.q: What did Israel find when they arrived at Elim?

options.text: Twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees.
options.note: Verse 27 gives these exact numbers, a place of rest and abundance.

options.text: A dry desert with no water at all.
options.note: Elim is described as a place of abundant water and shade, the opposite of a dry desert.

options.text: A hostile army waiting to attack them.
options.note: No army or conflict is mentioned at Elim; it is a place of rest.

options.text: Nothing of note; the text gives no description.
options.note: Verse 27 gives a specific, vivid description of abundance at Elim.

questions: Israel grumbled just three days after singing God's praises at the sea. How quickly does your own gratitude fade when the next hardship comes, even after clear evidence of God's care?

God did not rebuke Israel's need for water; He met it, even while testing their hearts in the process. Where might God be provided for you in a real need while also using the situation to test and grow your trust?
God revealed a new name of Himself, the LORD who heals, exactly at the point of Israel's helplessness. What part of your life right now most needs to know God specifically as healer?
After the bitterness of Marah came the abundance of Elim, twelve springs and seventy palm trees. Can you look back and see a similarly generous provision that followed a hard season in your own life?
The testing at Marah was meant to reveal what was really in Israel's heart. What does your typical response to minor inconveniences reveal about the depth of your trust in God?

translationNotes.note: Marah is the Hebrew word for bitter; the place name functions as a direct commentary on both the water and, implicitly, the people's complaint.
Statute and ordinance likely refers to a general principle of covenant obedience established here, prior to the fuller law given later at Sinai, rather than a specific detailed regulation.
I am the LORD who heals you renders the Hebrew Yahweh Rapha, a covenant name that became foundational to later Israelite and Christian confession of God's character as healer.
